scenario: Beacon Fintech publishes SLA dashboards across 20000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 and uses a FEEL division expression on two duration values to compute throughput multipliers inside a service task input mapping for a downstream reporting worker.
question: Which result type does the FEEL division operator return when dividing two days-time-duration operands of the same family?

options.text: A number value equal to the ratio of the two durations, as in duration("P5D") / duration("P1D") evaluating to 5 in the FEEL engine
A days-time-duration value equal to the remainder of the two operands after integer-style division performed internally by the FEEL engine
A years-months-duration value computed by converting both operands to months using a 30-day approximation inside the FEEL engine at runtime
A null value since the division table does not allow two duration operands of the same family as inputs to the FEEL division operator

a.text: Correct. The Division table maps days-time-duration / days-time-duration to a number, illustrated by duration("P5D") / duration("P1D") evaluating to 5.

b.text: Incorrect. The result of dividing two days-time-duration values is a number, not a remaining duration; the division table documents no integer-remainder semantic.

c.text: Incorrect. Cross-family conversion is not part of the division table; same-family duration / duration yields a number, and 30-day approximations are not documented.

d.text: Incorrect. The Division table explicitly allows duration / duration within the same family and returns a number, with examples such as duration("P1Y") / duration("P1M") evaluating to 12.

explanation: FEEL Division is defined for duration / duration within the same family, returning a number, and for duration / number, returning the same duration type. So duration("P5D") / duration("P1D") yields 5, while duration("P5D") / 5 yields duration("P1D").
